segments.title: Why the G3's third turret troubles this design
segments.summary: The G3's amidships third turret illustrates the tension between compact engineering and a battleship's usable arcs of fire.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that the third G3 turret is hemmed in by the superstructure and secondary battery, limiting its ability to engage targets ahead. He accepts that the arrangement has engineering logic, but judges the combat value too low for the weight and cost it consumes. The comparison leads him toward layouts that give every heavy turret a more useful field of fire.

evidence.note: He critiques the G3 third turret's restricted forward firing arc and combat utility.

segments.title: The cost of a quadruple-turret G3 hull
segments.summary: A visually attractive quadruple-turret concept exposes how quickly a G3-derived hull can become impractically heavy.
segments.body: The first game design grew to roughly 80,000 tons once its desired armament and fittings were included. Dr. Clarke instead presents a three-twin-16-inch arrangement on the G3-style hull, using the exercise to show how displacement limits discipline a designer's ambitions. The choice is less about a single ideal calibre than about keeping the whole ship workable.

evidence.note: He contrasts an 80,000-ton quadruple-turret version with a three-twin-16-inch alternative.

segments.title: Turret arcs, armour layout, and damage resilience
segments.summary: Widely usable turret arcs can justify a longer hull, while gunhouse placement also affects how blast and flooding loads travel through it.
segments.body: The proposed arrangement lets each main turret rotate through a full circle, which Dr. Clarke sees as valuable in a confused close-range action. He acknowledges that spreading the armament lengthens the ship and reduces concentration of heavy weapons. He also links large armoured turret structures and tight internal subdivision to the way torpedo damage energy may be concentrated rather than dissipated through the hull.

evidence.note: The discussion connects 360-degree turret training, ship length, and torpedo-damage effects.

segments.title: A 1920s six-inch dual-purpose secondary battery
segments.summary: Twin six-inch mounts become a design argument for investing early in a high-angle secondary battery rather than accepting later compromises.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke selects twin six-inch turrets comparable to contemporary cruiser fittings and imagines them receiving much higher elevation. A six-inch weapon able to reach 70 degrees could serve as a more capable dual-purpose battery if developed in the 1920s. The counterfactual is framed as one route that might reduce the need for the later Dido-style solution.

evidence.note: He explains his choice of twin six-inch turrets and the appeal of a 70-degree dual-purpose mount.

segments.title: Separating game fittings from a plausible 1920s ship
segments.summary: A combat-game design carries radar, sonar, and advanced machinery that make sense in play but distort a 1920s displacement calculation.
segments.body: The fully equipped version displaces roughly 70,000 tons after allowing for a long high-speed range, heavy protection, detection equipment, and advanced gun machinery. Dr. Clarke stresses that radar, sonar, autoloaders, and electro-hydraulic turrets are game-era necessities rather than an honest early-1920s fit. Removing those assumptions is the first step toward turning an entertaining design into a historical counterfactual.

evidence.note: He identifies the game-only fittings that add weight before asking what could be built in reality.

segments.title: Combining Hood's reach with Renown's cruising speed
segments.summary: The realistic concept is defined by a concrete operational requirement: roughly Hood's range while cruising at Renown's faster speed.
segments.body: The revised design is assessed at about 56,000 to 57,000 tons full load, rather than by the game's optimistic range figure. Its intended 8,000-nautical-mile radius at 18 knots matches Hood's reach while improving on Hood's cruising speed and retaining the Renown class's speed endurance. This turns speed and range from brochure figures into a fleet-use requirement for a fast battleship.

evidence.note: He derives the concept's displacement and operating range from Hood and Renown comparisons.

segments.title: Why a 1920s hull grows heavier by the Second World War
segments.summary: Protection, auxiliary machinery, and later anti-aircraft fittings explain why a plausible early fast battleship gains substantial weight over time.
segments.body: The realistic version keeps torpedo defence, reinforced subdivision, conventional loading gear, and a Hood-like power plant, but leaves out radar and sonar. Dr. Clarke then trims belt and deck protection while preserving turret armour, presenting a ship equipped for Second World War service rather than frozen at launch. The design illustrates the cumulative displacement pressure created by modernization rather than a single dramatic change.

evidence.note: He contrasts the stripped early fit with wartime fittings and revised armour thicknesses.

segments.title: A general-purpose battleship is not a duel-only design
segments.summary: The secondary battery and anti-aircraft outfit make sense in a balanced fleet action, even if a battleship-only duel rewards heavier main guns.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that a game restricted to battleship duels encourages designers to discard secondary armament and seek the largest or fastest-firing main battery possible. A more realistic task-group fight includes cruisers and destroyers, giving the six-inch and pom-pom batteries a distinct purpose. He links this balance to the Queen Elizabeth class as a successful model of the broadly useful fast battleship.

evidence.note: He contrasts a duel-optimized main battery with a general-purpose ship intended for fleet action.

segments.title: The Queen Elizabeth class as an unrealized fast-battleship idea
segments.summary: Higher-powered boilers could have made the Queen Elizabeth class a far more disruptive 28-knot fast-battleship proposition.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the Queen Elizabeths as the first fast battleships, but argues that contemporary technology was not developed as fully as it might have been. Small-tube boilers and extra power could, in his view, have pushed the class toward 28 knots. That potential informs the speed requirement of his own early-1920s concept.

evidence.note: He argues that more developed boiler technology could have given the Queen Elizabeth class 28 knots.

segments.title: G3, N3, and the blurred battlecruiser label
segments.summary: The G3 and N3 concepts reveal how a fast battleship combines battlecruiser mobility with enough armour for a fleet engagement.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sees the older contrast between commerce-hunting battlecruisers and battle-fighting battleships as too rigid for the G3 and N3 era. Most naval work demands mobility, while the rarer fleet action still demands a ship that can survive serious gunfire. His definition of the fast battleship is therefore a battlecruiser concept with the armour needed for that wider role.

evidence.note: He describes fast battleships as a hybrid of battlecruiser mobility and battleship protection.

segments.title: Testing the design against a Regia Marina-style force
segments.summary: A Mediterranean-style game battle tests the design as part of a mixed force, not as an isolated capital ship.
segments.body: The combat test pairs the proposed battleship, modified Town-class cruisers, and Tribal-class destroyers against an Italian force with a battleship and cruisers. Dr. Clarke chooses the Regia Marina because its early-war surface doctrine fits a carrier-less Mediterranean comparison better than a Japanese scenario. The six-inch secondary battery keeps enemy light cruisers at range, while the wider force fight exposes both the value and limits of the design.

evidence.note: He describes a game test against an Italian surface force and its mixed cruiser-destroyer setting.

segments.title: A shorter armour belt as the final compromise
segments.summary: Bringing the guns closer together shortens the protected length, accepting some firing-arc limits to release weight and growth margin.
segments.body: The final version is slightly shorter because its main turrets are brought closer together and the armoured belt is reduced in length. B turret retains a full circle of training, but the other positions sacrifice some arcs. Dr. Clarke considers that an acceptable compromise because the hull gains space and capacity for later improvements without abandoning the core fast-battleship idea.

evidence.note: He explains the reduced armoured-belt length and the firing-arc compromise in the final design.
